toolSpecification.description:
Execute the specified shell (CMD or Powershell) command.

# Rules
- Use grep_search, file_search, read_file, and read_files for search and discovery instead of cat, find, grep, or ls.
- Use fs_write for file writing instead of mkdir or piping (folders are managed for you).
- Use the 'cwd' parameter to run commands in subdirectories. The 'cd' command is not supported and will fail.
- Use the control_pwsh_process tool with action "start" for long-running commands like development servers (npm run dev, yarn start), build watchers (webpack --watch), or interactive commands (vim, nano), as they can block execution.
- If you are certain a command will complete quickly despite triggering a warning, you can use ignoreWarning: true to bypass the check.
- Use paths relative to the current working directory in commands. BAD: cwd: src, command: mkdir src/tests GOOD: cwd: src, command: mkdir tests
- When using inline interpreters (python -c, node -e, bash -c), keep commands to a SINGLE LINE. If the logic requires multiple lines or complex quoting, write to a temporary script file and execute it instead.
- AVOID using ! inside double-quoted strings (e.g. grep "!important"). Bash history expansion interprets ! in double quotes. Use single quotes instead: grep '!important'.
- AVOID heredoc syntax (cat << 'EOF') for creating files. Use the file writing tool instead.

anyOf.description: Absolute path to the working directory. The command will run in this directory. Defaults to workspace root if omitted.
Absolute path to the working directory. The command will run in this directory. Defaults to workspace root if omitted.

cwd.description: Absolute path to the working directory. The command will run in this directory. Defaults to workspace root if omitted.

anyOf.description: Set to true to bypass long-running command warnings. Use only when you are certain the command will complete quickly.
Set to true to bypass long-running command warnings. Use only when you are certain the command will complete quickly.

ignoreWarning.description: Set to true to bypass long-running command warnings. Use only when you are certain the command will complete quickly.

anyOf.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds for command execution. If not specified, commands will run without a timeout (infinite). If the command exceeds the specified timeout, the latest output will be returned and the agent can continue.

Optional timeout in milliseconds for command execution. If not specified, commands will run without a timeout (infinite). If the command exceeds the specified timeout, the latest output will be returned and the agent can continue.

timeout.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds for command execution. If not specified, commands will run without a timeout (infinite). If the command exceeds the specified timeout, the latest output will be returned and the agent can continue.

anyOf.description: Optional warning to display to the user alongside the command execution. Only use this field when running property based tests or running test suites that contain property based tests
Optional warning to display to the user alongside the command execution. Only use this field when running property based tests or running test suites that contain property based tests

warning.description: Optional warning to display to the user alongside the command execution. Only use this field when running property based tests or running test suites that contain property based tests

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified command output. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most commands.

anyOf.description: One sentence explanation of why this command is being run and what information you expect from the output. This helps intelligently prune large outputs.

toolSpecification.name: execute_pwsh

toolSpecification.description:
Tool for listing directory contents. Content is returned in the "long format" similar to `ls -la`.

Example Usage:
1. List the files in the home directory: path="~"
2. Recursively list files in a directory to a max depth of 2: path="/path/to/directory", depth=2

path.description: Path to directory, e.g. `/project/src` or `/project/repo`. Can also start with ~ for the user's home.

explanation.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal.

anyOf.description: Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional)
Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional)

depth.description: Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional)

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified directory listing. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most reads.

toolSpecification.name: list_directory

toolSpecification.description:
Tool for reading a single file with optional line range specification. This tool reads the content of one file and can optionally limit the output to specific line ranges.

Example Usage:
- Read entire file: path="file.txt"
- Read specific lines: path="file.txt", start_line=5, end_line=10
- Read from line 5 to end: path="file.txt", start_line=5
- Read first 10 lines: path="file.txt", end_line=10

Rules:
1. Use line ranges to focus on relevant sections of large files only when absolutely necessary. Prefer reading entire files.
2. For reading multiple files, use the read_files tool instead

Content Pruning:
By default, large files are intelligently pruned to extract only the most relevant sections based on your explanation. This is the preferred behavior for most reads.
Only set skipPruning=true when you genuinely need the complete unmodified file. Do not overuse skipPruning.
If you need the full file, use skipPruning=true instead of writing "need complete file" or similar in the explanation field.

path.description: Path to file to read. e.g. '/project/file.py' or '/project/src/main.ts', or in ~/.kiro

anyOf.description: Starting line number (optional).
Starting line number (optional).
Ending line number (optional).
Ending line number (optional).

explanation.description: One or two sentences explaining as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune file content, so provide context about the current task and what specific information you need from the file.

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified file content. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most reads.

toolSpecification.name: read_file

toolSpecification.description:
Tool for reading multiple files with optional line range specification. This tool reads the content of multiple files and can optionally limit the output to specific line ranges.

Example Usage:
- Read multiple files: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"]
- Read specific lines from multiple files: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"], start_line=5, end_line=10
- Read from line 5 to end: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"], start_line=5
- Read first 10 lines: paths=["file1.txt", "file2.txt"], end_line=10

Rules:
1. Reading multiple files at once is preferred over multiple single-file reads
2. Use line ranges to focus on relevant sections of large files only when absolutely necessary. Prefer reading entire files.
3. For reading a single file, use the read_file tool instead

Content Pruning:
By default, large files are intelligently pruned to extract only the most relevant sections based on your explanation. This is the preferred behavior for most reads.
Only set skipPruning=true when you genuinely need the complete unmodified file (e.g., for copying, moving, or full-file refactoring). Do not overuse skipPruning.
If you need the full file, use skipPruning=true instead of writing "need complete file" or similar in the explanation field.

paths.description: Paths to files to read. e.g. ['/project/file.py', '/project/src/main.ts'], or in ~/.kiro

anyOf.description: Starting line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.
Starting line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

start_line.description: Starting line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

anyOf.description: Ending line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.
Ending line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

end_line.description: Ending line number (optional). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

explanation.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune file content, so provide context about the current task and what specific information you need from the files.

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified file content. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most reads.

toolSpecification.name: read_files

toolSpecification.description: Fast file search based on fuzzy matching against file path. Use if you know part of the file path but don't know where it's located exactly. Response will be capped to 10 results. Make your query more specific if need to filter results further.

query.description: The regex pattern to search for

anyOf.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude
Glob pattern for files to exclude

excludePattern.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude

anyOf.description: Whether the search should include .gitignore files, should be the string yes or no
Whether the search should include .gitignore files, should be the string yes or no

includeIgnoredFiles.description: Whether the search should include .gitignore files, should be the string yes or no

toolSpecification.name: file_search

toolSpecification.description:
Fast text-based regex search that finds exact pattern matches within files or directories using ripgrep.
Search results include line numbers, file paths, and 2 lines of context around each match.

Parameters:
- query (required): The regex pattern to search for. Use Rust regex syntax.
- caseSensitive (optional): "yes" for case-sensitive search, "no" or omit for case-insensitive
- includePattern (optional): Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts', 'src/**/*.js'). Use ** for recursive search. If omitted, searches all files in workspace
- excludePattern (optional): Glob pattern for files to exclude (e.g. '*.log', 'node_modules/**')
- explanation (optional): Brief description of why this search is being performed

Search Behavior:
- Use includePattern or excludePattern to filter the search scope by file type or specific paths. If no includePattern is specified, the tool will search all files.
- The results are capped at 100 matches and long lines are truncated with "[truncated: line too long]" indicator.
- If total output is too large, truncates with "[truncated: too many matches]" message.

Examples of VALID patterns to use as "query":
- Basic text search: "function", "error", "TODO"
- Word boundaries: "\bword\b" (matches 'word' but not 'password')
- Multiple words: "auth.*failed"
- File content with spaces: "not found"
- Line starts with: "^import"
- Line ends with: "};$"
- Numbers: "\d+\.\d+" (finds decimal numbers like 3.14)
- Word followed by another: "function\s+\w+" (finds function declarations)

Rules:
1. Keep regex patterns simple. Complex patterns may fail.
2. Use includePattern to narrow search scope for better performance
3. Glob patterns: use standard glob syntax (* for wildcards, ** for recursive). Always use ** for recursive search (e.g. "**/*.py" not "*.py")
4. Prefer this tool over bash grep, as it is optimized for your machine and provides structured results.
5. Always escape special regex characters: ( ) [ ] { } + * ? ^ $ | . \
6. You MUST use \\ to escape any of special regex characters when they appear in your search string.

query.description: The regex pattern to search for

anyOf.description: Whether the search should be case sensitive
Whether the search should be case sensitive

caseSensitive.description: Whether the search should be case sensitive

anyOf.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude
Glob pattern for files to exclude

excludePattern.description: Glob pattern for files to exclude

anyOf.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune search results.
One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune search results.

explanation.description: One sentence explanation as to why this tool is being used, and how it contributes to the goal. This will be used to intelligently prune search results.

anyOf.description: Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts' for TypeScript files). Use ** for recursive search
Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts' for TypeScript files). Use ** for recursive search

includePattern.description: Glob pattern for files to include (e.g. '**/*.ts' for TypeScript files). Use ** for recursive search

skipPruning.description: Only set to true when you need the complete unmodified search results. Default (false) allows intelligent pruning which is preferred for most searches.

toolSpecification.name: grep_search

toolSpecification.description:
Report progress on the current task. Use this tool every 4-5 turns to inform about your current status.
Progress reports should be concise (1-2 sentences) and describe what you're working on.
Examples:
- "Analyzed 3 files for security issues, found 2 potential vulnerabilities"
- "Completed file analysis, now generating test cases"
- "Refactored 5 components, running tests to verify changes"

message.description: Progress update message describing current work (1-2 sentences)

toolSpecification.name: report_progress

response.description: Text response for the orchestrator summarizing your work. Can be empty if files provide sufficient context.

files.description: List of files with optional line ranges to include in parent context

toolSpecification.name: subagent_response

userInputMessage.content:
# Efficient Context Gathering Agent

You are a context gathering specialist that identifies and retrieves the minimal, sufficient information needed to address user queries.

## Your Mission

Intelligently gather ONLY essential code sections using the cheapest effective approach. Maximize parallel operations and batch file reads.

## Available Context

You have been provided with the following context to help you understand the codebase structure:

### File Tree
#[[fileTree]]

### Currently Open Files
#[[currentlyOpenFiles:all]]

## Step 1: Analyze Available Context (BEFORE any tool calls)

Check what's already provided above:
- **File tree** - Already provided, use it to identify relevant directories and files
- **Open files** - Shows what the user is currently working on
- **Documentation** - Check README, docs folders for high-level understanding
- **Package files** - package.json, tsconfig.json for project structure

## Step 2: Choose Strategy Based on Query

**Clear file names + descriptive query:**
→ Direct batch read (1 tool call)
Example: "Fix login validation" + auth/login_validator.ts
Tools: readMultipleFiles([file1, file2, ...])

**Specific symbols mentioned:**
→ Targeted search then batch read (2 tool calls)
Example: "Where is UserService.authenticate?"
Tools: grepSearch → readMultipleFiles

**Documentation available:**
→ Check docs first (1-2 tool calls)
Example: "How does rate limiting work?" + README.md present
Tools: readFile(README.md) or grepSearch → readMultipleFiles if needed

**Vague query + unclear structure:**
→ Explore structure then targeted reads (2-3 tool calls)
Tools: listDirectory → fileSearch/grepSearch → readMultipleFiles

## Tool Usage Guidelines

**ALWAYS BATCH - Never sequential reads:**
✅ readMultipleFiles([file1, file2, file3]) - 1 call
❌ readFile(file1) → readFile(file2) → readFile(file3) - 3 calls

**ALWAYS PARALLEL - Make independent calls together:**
✅ Call grepSearch + readFile(README) in same turn if independent
❌ Wait for grep results before reading README if unrelated

**Tool Cost Hierarchy (cheap to expensive):**
1. **CHEAP**: fileSearch, grepSearch, executeBash (quick shell commands)
2. **MODERATE**: readFile, readMultipleFiles (prefer batched)
3. **EXPENSIVE**: readCode (AST-based, use when symbol relationships needed)

**When to use each tool:**
- readMultipleFiles: Primary tool - batch read 2-5 pre-identified files
- readFile: Only for single file (prefer batching)
- fileSearch/grepSearch: Locate specific symbols/patterns before reading
- executeBash: Quick checks (file existence, line counts, git log)
- readCode: Only when need AST-based symbol analysis
- getDiagnostics: Check for errors in specific files

## Tool Call Budget

**Target: 1-2 calls** (most queries with clear signals)
**Acceptable: 3 calls** (complex queries, cheap operations)
**Maximum: 4 calls** (only if truly necessary)

## Decision Framework

1. Are file names descriptive for this query?
   → YES: Batch read obvious candidates (1 call)
   → NO: Continue to step 2

2. Does query mention specific symbols/functions?
   → YES: Search then batch read (2 calls)
   → NO: Continue to step 3

3. Are there README/docs files?
   → YES: Read/grep docs first (1-2 calls)
   → NO: Continue to step 4

4. Can you identify 2-4 likely files from structure?
   → YES: Batch read them (1 call)
   → NO: Use search tools to narrow down (2-3 calls)

## Critical Rules

1. **Batch all file reads** - Use readMultipleFiles whenever reading 2+ files
2. **Parallel independent operations** - Make unrelated tool calls together
3. **Cheap before expensive** - Try search/docs before expensive operations
4. **Extract minimal content** - Only sections directly related to query
5. **Maximum 2-5 files** - Be highly selective
6. **Provide actionable context** - Focus on what's needed to solve the problem

## Output Format

You MUST call the subagentResponse tool as your final action.

For each relevant file, specify the file path and the precise line ranges that contain relevant code.
Use the files parameter with path, startLine, and endLine for each section.

**IMPORTANT:**
- Include ONLY the relevant parts of files - not entire files
- The same file can be referenced multiple times with different startLine/endLine combinations
- Use precise line ranges to include only the code sections that matter

Example subagentResponse call:
{
  "response": "Found authentication implementation and validation logic",
  "files": [
    { "path": "src/auth/login.ts", "startLine": 10, "endLine": 45 },
    { "path": "src/auth/login.ts", "startLine": 80, "endLine": 120 },
    { "path": "src/auth/validator.ts", "startLine": 5, "endLine": 35 }
  ]
}

**Goal: Minimum tool calls, maximum information gain, intelligent cost management.**
<progress_reporting>
- You MUST use the reportProgress tool every 4-5 turns to update on your current status
- Keep progress messages concise (1-2 sentences)
- Focus on what you're currently working on and any significant findings
- Examples:
  - "Reviewed authentication module, identified 2 security concerns"
  - "Completed file analysis, now generating test cases"
  - "Refactored 5 components, running tests to verify changes"
- This helps track long-running tasks and provides visibility to the parent agent
</progress_reporting>

<response_requirement>
- You MUST use the subagentResponse tool to communicate your final result back to the parent agent
- This is REQUIRED - do not end your execution without calling subagentResponse
- Include a clear, concise summary of what you accomplished in the response parameter
- If you found relevant files that the parent agent should see, include them in the files parameter
- The parent agent cannot see your work unless you call subagentResponse
</response_requirement>

  }
}

<file name="f:\AUDIRA-Cloud-Device-Orchestrator\README.md" language="markdown" >
<content>
# Cloud Device Orchestrator

Enterprise platform for controlling thousands of devices in real-time with AI-powered automation, fleet management, and distributed command execution.

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Global CDN / WAF                          │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
┌──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┐
│                   API Gateway (NestJS)                        │
│              WebSocket / MQTT / gRPC Gateway                  │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
┌──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Microservices Layer                          │
│  Auth │ Device │ Command │ Telemetry │ AI │ Workflow │ OTA   │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
┌──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Data Platform                                │
│  PostgreSQL │ Redis │ Kafka │ ClickHouse │ Elasticsearch     │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
┌──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Edge Device Agents                           │
│  Android │ Linux │ IoT │ Docker │ Browser │ Kubernetes       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Tech Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|-------|-----------|
| Frontend | Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript, TailwindCSS |
| UI Design | Neo Brutalism + Cyberpunk + Enterprise Fusion |
| State | Zustand, TanStack Query |
| Backend | NestJS, Fastify, TypeScript |
| Realtime | Socket.IO, MQTT, WebRTC |
| Queue | Kafka, NATS |
| Database | PostgreSQL 16 |
| Cache | Redis 7 |
| Analytics | ClickHouse |
| Search | Elasticsearch |
| AI | OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama |
| Infrastructure | Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform |
| CI/CD | GitHub Actions, ArgoCD |
| Monitoring | Prometheus, Grafana, Loki |

## Monorepo Structure

```
/apps
  /web              → Next.js frontend (landing + dashboard)
  /gateway          → NestJS API gateway
  /ai-engine        → AI orchestration service (planned)
  /mobile           → React Native mobile app (planned)
  /desktop          → Tauri desktop app (planned)

/packages
  /ui               → Shared UI component library
  /shared-types     → TypeScript type definitions
  /sdk              → Client SDK (planned)

/services
  /device-service   → Device management microservice
  /auth-service     → Authentication service (planned)
  /telemetry-service → Telemetry processing (planned)

/infrastructure
  /docker           → Docker configs & init scripts
  /kubernetes       → K8s manifests
  /terraform        → IaC for cloud provisioning
```

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites

- Node.js >= 22
- pnpm >= 9.15
- Docker & Docker Compose

### Development Setup

```bash
# Clone the repository
git clone <repo-url>
cd cloud-device-orchestrator

# Install dependencies
pnpm install

# Start infrastructure (PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, etc.)
docker compose up -d

# Start all services in development mode
pnpm dev
```

### Individual Services

```bash
# Frontend only
pnpm --filter @orchestrator/web dev

# API Gateway only
pnpm --filter @orchestrator/gateway dev

# Device Service only
pnpm --filter @orchestrator/device-service dev
```

## API Documentation

Once the gateway is running, Swagger docs are available at:
```
http://localhost:4000/api/docs
```

## Key Features

- **Fleet Management** — Monitor and control 10,000+ devices
- **AI Orchestration** — Natural language device control with multi-agent system
- **Real-time Control** — WebRTC remote screen, shell access, file transfer
- **Automation Builder** — Visual drag-and-drop workflow engine
- **OTA Updates** — Canary deployments with rollback
- **Telemetry** — Live metrics with AI anomaly detection
- **Multi-Tenant** — Enterprise SaaS with RBAC
- **Plugin Ecosystem** — Extensible marketplace
- **Edge Computing** — Offline-first with edge sync

## SaaS Plans

| Feature | Free | Pro | Enterprise |
|---------|------|-----|-----------|
| Devices | 5 | 100 | Unlimited |
| AI Automation | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| OTA Updates | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| Analytics | Basic | Full | Full + Custom |
| Support | Community | Email | Dedicated + SLA |
| White-label | — | — | ✓ |

## Security

- OAuth2 + Passkeys + MFA
- Zero-trust networking with mTLS
- API Gateway with rate limiting & WAF
- Encrypted at rest and in transit
- SOC2 / ISO27001 / GDPR compliant architecture

## License

Proprietary — All rights reserved.

</content>
</file>
<file name="f:\AUDIRA-Cloud-Device-Orchestrator\.env.example" language="dotenv" >

# ============================================
# Cloud Device Orchestrator - Environment Config
# ============================================

# Application
NODE_ENV=development
APP_PORT=3387
API_PORT=3388
GATEWAY_PORT=3388

# Database - PostgreSQL
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/orchestrator
DATABASE_POOL_SIZE=20

# Redis
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379
REDIS_PASSWORD=

# Kafka
KAFKA_BROKERS=localhost:9092
KAFKA_CLIENT_ID=orchestrator

# NATS
NATS_URL=nats://localhost:4222

# ClickHouse
CLICKHOUSE_URL=http://localhost:8123
CLICKHOUSE_DATABASE=telemetry

# Elasticsearch
ELASTICSEARCH_URL=http://localhost:9200

# MinIO / S3
S3_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:9000
S3_ACCESS_KEY=minioadmin
S3_SECRET_KEY=minioadmin
S3_BUCKET=orchestrator

# MQTT
MQTT_BROKER_URL=mqtt://localhost:1883
MQTT_USERNAME=
MQTT_PASSWORD=

# Auth
JWT_SECRET=your-super-secret-jwt-key-change-in-production
JWT_EXPIRATION=3600
REFRESH_TOKEN_EXPIRATION=604800

# AI Providers
OPENAI_API_KEY=
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
GEMINI_API_KEY=

# Stripe Billing
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=
STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET=

# WebRTC
TURN_SERVER_URL=
TURN_USERNAME=
TURN_PASSWORD=

# Monitoring
GRAFANA_URL=http://localhost:3001
PROMETHEUS_URL=http://localhost:9090

</content>

<file name="f:\AUDIRA-Cloud-Device-Orchestrator\docker-compose.yml" language="dockercompose" >
<content>
version: '3.8'

services:
  # PostgreSQL Database
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    container_name: orchestrator-postgres
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: orchestrator
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: orchestrator_secret
      POSTGRES_DB: orchestrator
    ports:
      - "5433:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U orchestrator"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  # Redis Cache & Session Store
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    container_name: orchestrator-redis
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "6380:6379"
    volumes:
      - redis_data:/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  # ClickHouse Analytics Database
  clickhouse:
    image: clickhouse/clickhouse-server:latest
    container_name: orchestrator-clickhouse
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      CLICKHOUSE_DB: orchestrator
      CLICKHOUSE_USER: orchestrator
      CLICKHOUSE_PASSWORD: clickhouse_secret
      CLICKHOUSE_DEFAULT_ACCESS_MANAGEMENT: 1
    ports:
      - "8123:8123"
      - "9000:9000"
    volumes:
      - clickhouse_data:/var/lib/clickhouse
      - ./clickhouse-init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8123/ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  redis_data:
  clickhouse_data:

</content>
</file>

Reading main entry points and key configuration files to understand current implementation status and features
